data.title: Katy Perry accompanies Trudeau in Davos
data.description:
Katy Perry was seen Tuesday alongside former Canadian Prime Minister Justin Trudeau at the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland.

Trudeau was spotted in a dark blue suit, while Perry was wearing a modest tan skirt set with a sweater. The two made their relationship official last October while the pop star was still on her “Lifetimes” tour which spanned across Budapest, Hungary; Kraków, Poland; Prague, Czech Republic, and Munich.

Trudeau, who resigned from office in March, separated from his ex-wife Sophie Gregoire in 2023, after 18 years of marriage. Perry broke off her long engagement to actor Orlando Bloom last year.

People, citing a source close to Perry, reported Sunday that the pop star “didn’t expect to fall for somebody so soon” after ending her engagement, but Trudeau “has a great sense of humor, he’s charming and treats her with respect.”

The lovers marked their territory in an official Instagram post last December with Perry showing herself and the former Canadian prime minister together in Japan. 

She captioned the post, “Tokyo times on tour and more,” and added a smiling face along with other emojis, including flowers and a red heart.

Trudeau also reposted a photo of the two on social platform X with Japan’s former prime minister, Fumio Kishida, and his wife, Yuko Kishida.

data.title: FTC plans to appeal Meta monopoly decision
data.description:
The Federal Trade Commission (FTC) said Tuesday that it plans to appeal a November ruling finding that Meta did not have a monopoly over personal social networking.

The agency, which sued the Facebook parent in 2020, suffered a key loss late last year when a federal judge determined the tech giant competes in a broader market with companies like TikTok and YouTube.

“Our position has not changed,” said Joe Simonson, FTC’s director of public affairs. “Meta violated our antitrust laws when it acquired Instagram and WhatsApp. Consequently, American consumers have suffered from Meta’s monopoly.”

“The staggering market power was on full display for everyone to see in 2020,” he continued. “Thankfully for Americans, there is an appeal process. We intend to file a notice of appeal to the U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals for the D.C. Circuit later today, and continue our fight for competitive markets and innovation.”

The FTC sued Meta in the first Trump administration for allegedly entrenching its social networking monopoly with its acquisitions of Instagram and WhatsApp.

However, U.S. District Judge James Boasberg underscored in his decision in November that the social media landscape has “changed markedly” since the agency first brought its case.

“Whether or not Meta enjoyed monopoly power in the past, though, the agency must show that it continues to hold such power now,” Boasberg wrote at the time. “The Court’s verdict today determines that the FTC has not done so.”

A senior FTC official argued Tuesday that the judge erred in his decision. The agency contends that TikTok and YouTube are not part of the same market as Meta’s products, which they suggest are instead focused on sharing and seeing content from family and friends.

The Hill has reached out to Meta for comment.

data.title: Netflix tops 325 million paid subscribers
data.description:
Netflix’s subscription business is booming.

The company said it had surpassed 325 million subscribers worldwide during a presentation to investors on Tuesday, increasing its operating income by 30 percent thanks to new customers added in the final quarter of 2025.

Revenues increased more than 15 percent in the last quarter of 2025, the company said, telling investors it plans to spend aggressively in the coming year on content creation and assets acquisition.

The most prominent of these goals is the company’s bid for Warner Bros. Discovery, the sprawling movie and television studio which Netflix is eyeing to buy with an all-cash bid totaling more than $70 billion.

The Netflix-Warner Bros. deal has raised concern among lawmakers and critics of media consolidation and will need to clear President Trump’s government regulators.

Media conglomerate Paramount has mounted a hostile bid for Warner Bros. Discovery, with top executives at the company lobbying Trump to support its push for the company over that of Netflix.

Netflix has made an aggressive push into pro sports, video podcasting and other live events as it seeks to set itself apart from fellow tech giants Amazon and Google, which have similarly poured hundreds of millions into their direct-to-consumer streaming businesses in recent years.

data.title: US forces apprehend 7th oil tanker in Caribbean
data.description:
The U.S. forces apprehended their seventh oil tanker in the Caribbean on Tuesday as the Trump administration continues to enforce its quarantine of sanctioned vessels in the region. 

The U.S. personnel, made up of officials from the U.S. Coast Guard, along with the U.S. Navy and Marines, seized the Motor Vessel Sagitta “without incident,” according to the U.S. Southern Command (Southcom), which shared a 20-second clip of the operation. 

“The apprehension of another tanker operating in defiance of President Trump’s established quarantine of sanctioned vessels in the Caribbean demonstrates our resolve to ensure that the only oil leaving Venezuela will be oil that is coordinated properly and lawfully,” Southcom said in the post. 

“These operations are backed by the full power our elite joint force team deployed in the Caribbean,” Southcom added. 

Last Thursday, the U.S. forces seized their sixth Venezuela-linked oil tanker, Veronica, which is part of the so-called ghost fleet, in the Caribbean. 

Earlier this month, Secretary of State Marco Rubio indicated that the U.S. would not govern Venezuela, after U.S. Special Forces captured Venezuelan leader Nicolas Maduro, but said that the Trump administration would keep enforcing an existing “oil quarantine” on oil vessels coming in and out of the Latin American country.

data.title: Hogan says he won't run again for Maryland governor, other public office
data.description:
Former Maryland Gov. Larry Hogan (R) said Tuesday he will not run again for public office, ruling out another bid for his old seat and ending speculation about a possible return to politics.

“I care deeply about the state of Maryland, and I remain concerned about the direction of our nation’s politics, but I have no intention of running for office again,” Hogan wrote in an op-ed in The Baltimore Sun.

“It was an honor to serve, but it’s time to look forward, not back,” he continued. “The fight for Maryland and America’s future does not belong to any one person or any one party; it belongs to the people.”

The moderate Republican, who ran unsuccessfully for Senate in 2024, urged his party to embrace term limits on elected officials and look forward toward a new generation of leaders.

“To my fellow Republicans: It is time to invest in a new generation of candidates who are ready to step up, and as elected politics is a binary proposition, who are willing to win with a positive message that appeals to more people,” he wrote.

Hogan was governor from 2015 to 2023 and emerged as a rare Republican critic of President Trump’s during the president’s first term. In 2024, Hogan did not endorse either former President Biden or Trump.

Hogan’s social media account stirred rumors of a potential 2026 comeback bid, with a post several months ago including a photo of a “Hogan for Maryland” campaign bus and a message that suggested either selling the vehicle, adding, “Or…I guess we could always rewrap it and get back out on the road again?”

But, with his op-ed on Tuesday, the former governor made clear he would not try to win back his seat against Gov. Wes Moore (D) this year.

“I am enjoying being back in the private sector and spending more time with my family, but I am not leaving the conversation,” he wrote. “I will continue to speak out on issues that matter, especially when our record has proven a better path forward. And I will be working to inspire and support the next generation of civic leaders.”

data.title: Trump blames 'bad public relations people' on not selling economic wins
data.description:
President Trump on Tuesday acknowledged his administration was having trouble selling his economic wins, at one point saying it could be the result of “bad public relations people.”

“The numbers that we inherited were way up, and now we brought them, almost all of them, way down, way down,” Trump told reporters from the White House briefing room where he made an appearance to mark exactly one year in office.

“I mean… maybe you have bad public relations people, but we’re not getting it across. We inherited high numbers, and we brought them way down,” he continued, appearing to lay the blame on his staff for not selling his agenda effectively to the public.

Trump has struggled with poll numbers when it comes to how Americans are feeling on the economy with stubborn inflation holding steady at 2.7.

But the president insisted his policies have brought down costs, including on gas and eggs. He has also mocked the notion of affordability as a way to push back on recent Democratic wins.

He has also repeatedly laid blame on former President Biden for any economic woes being felt by voters who continue to express concern over the prices of everyday goods and food.

data.title: Americans paying almost entirely for Trump tariffs: Study
data.description:
A new study from the Kiel Institute for the World Economy found that Americans bore 96 percent of the costs incurred by President Trump’s tariffs last year. 

The study, which analyzed shipment-level data covering over 25 million transactions, found that firms importing goods into the U.S. absorbed only 4 percent of the costs from the levies. The remaining 96 percent was passed on to American buyers, while U.S. customs revenue surged by roughly $200 billion last year.

The Kiel Institute noted that firms passed on tariff costs to buyers due to, among other reasons, the existence of other markets in Europe, Asia or elsewhere and difficulties switching suppliers. 

Trump unveiled sweeping tariffs on foreign partners last April, imposing a baseline 10 percent tax on all imports and higher rates for certain countries, such as China, and specific sectors, such as automobile parts, steel and aluminum. 

The president has since increased tariffs on imports from Brazil and India to 50 percent. He reversed course on Brazil in November, lifting levies on food imports, including coffee, fruit, cocoa and beef.

On Saturday, he threatened 10 percent tariffs starting Feb. 1 on imports from Denmark, Norway, Sweden, France, Germany, Finland, the U.K. and the Netherlands over their support for Greenland’s sovereignty. 

Trump added that the levies will increase to 25 percent on June 1, and will remain in place until Danish and Greenlandic officials agree to grant the U.S. control of the mineral-rich island.

Last month, inflation hit 2.7 percent, down slightly from 3 percent in January. Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell said in December that the president’s tariffs are causing “most” of the overshooting of the central bank’s 2 percent inflation target.

“We do think of those as likely to, in the current situation, likely to be a one-time … price increase,” Powell told reporters. 

The month prior, the Trump administration defended his widespread tariff authority in front of the Supreme Court. The president has cited the International Emergency Economic Powers Act — which grants him the authority to regulate economic transactions during national emergencies — to justify the import taxes. 

The administration’s argument, though, garnered skepticism from conservative and liberal justices alike in November. It is unclear when the high court will rule on the case, which was on an expedited timeline.

data.title: Usha Vance announces she's pregnant
data.description:
The Second Family is expanding: Usha Vance, wife of Vice President Vance, is pregnant.

“We’re very excited to share the news that Usha is pregnant with our fourth child, a boy,” the Vances said in a joint statement shared on social media on Tuesday.

“Usha and the baby are going well, and we are all looking forward to welcoming him in late July,” the statement said. 

The pair — who have been married since 2014 — expressed gratitude for “the military doctors who take excellent care of our family and for the staff members who do so much to ensure that we can serve the country while enjoying a wonderful life with our children.”

The Vances are parents to three young children: Ewan, Vivek and Mirabel. 

In December, the vice president said his marriage was “as strong as it’s ever been,” following online speculation about his relationship with his wife. 

“With anything in life, you take the good with the bad,” Vance said in an interview with NBC News. 

“You accept that there are some sacrifices and there are some very good things that come along with it, too,” Vance said, adding Usha had “really taken” to her role as second lady. 

In an interview with Fox News Digital last year, Usha Vance, a former trial lawyer, said that raising her brood in the public eye posed “new challenges.”

“While I certainly miss working, I am grateful that I have the time to ensure that our home life is as normal and stable as possible,” Vance, 40, said.

data.title: Trump on Greenland: 'We're going to work something out'
data.description:
President Trump said the U.S. and the North Atlantic Treaty Organization (NATO) would benefit from the U.S. acquiring Greenland. 

“I think we’re going to work something out where NATO’s going to be very happy and we’re going to be very happy. But we need it for security purposes,” Trump told reporters from the White House briefing room on Tuesday.

The comment came as Trump prepares to head to Davos, Switzerland for the World Economic Forum where he will come face to face with a number of NATO allies who have raised concerns over his intensifying ambitions for the U.S. to acquire Greenland. 

When asked at the briefing how far he would be willing to go to acquire the Danish territory, Trump said “you’ll find out.” 

Over the weekend, Trump levied 10 percent tariffs on Denmark and its European allies over Greenland. Additionally, the president has not ruled out military force to acquire the territory. 

The actions have led to unusually forceful pushback from a number of European leaders, including UK Prime Minister Kier Starmer and French President Emmanuel Macron. The French president is one of the few foreign leaders to be public about his criticisms more forceful, especially in recent days. 

Starmer issued a rare criticism of Trump over the issue, saying at an earlier press conference “any decision about the future status of Greenland belongs to the people of Greenland and the kingdom of Denmark alone.”

Trump on Tuesday shared a screenshot of a conversation with French President Emmanuel Macron in which Macron tells him, “I do not understand what you are doing on Greenland.” 

In response to a question from The Hill about how he gauges his relationship with Starmer and Macron, Trump said that he gets along “very well” with the two leaders. 

“They get a little bit rough when they’re, you know, when I’m not around, but when I’m around they treat me very nicely,” Trump said.

data.title: Whitmer breaks with Michelle Obama, says US 'ready for a woman president'
data.description:
Michigan Gov. Gretchen Whitmer (D) said she believes the U.S. is “ready for a woman president,” directly contrasting comments made by former first lady Michelle Obama.

During her book tour last fall, Obama said that the country was “not ready” for a female president, referencing former Vice President Harris’s loss to President Trump.

“As we saw in this past election, sadly, we ain’t ready,” the former first lady said.

When asked about these sentiments by NPR in an interview released on Tuesday, Whitmer expressed her “love” for Obama, saying, “the last thing I want to do is disagree with her.”

“But, you know, I think America is ready for a woman president,” Whitmer said. “The question comes down to a choice between two people, and what we saw in this last election, while Kamala Harris didn’t beat President Trump, we saw women get elected across the country.”

The governor referenced the recent election victories of Govs. Abigail Spanberger (D-Va.) and Mikie Sherill (D-N.J.) along with Sen. Elissa Slotkin (D-Mich.). 

“We saw women win up and down the ballot in hard, important states to win, so I do think there’s an appetite,” Whitmer said. “I just, for whatever reason, we have not had a woman president yet. I think we will at some point in the near future.”

When asked specifically whether the fact that Harris was a woman lost her the election, Whitmer said, “I don’t think it was just gender, no.”

Whitmer’s conversation with NPR largely centered on the midterms elections and American automotive manufacturing. Michigan has the largest numbers of auto workers in the U.S. 

“We know that tariffs have increased costs on the average American consumer, they’ve cost Americans jobs, they’ve made America a less competitive country as a whole and you can see what the toll is on American manufacturing,” Whitmer said. 

Additionally, Whitmer pushed back on questioning about the extent of the tariff impacts on American production, pointing to “nine months of contraction” in manufacturing processes.

“We’re the canary in the coal mine, if you will,” Whitmer said. “When America catches a cold, Michigan gets the flu. We feel it harder and we feel it earlier, and I’m telling you, it’s taking a toll.”

Whitmer, who is serving out her last term as governor, confirmed she doesn’t currently have plans to run for another seat. While she has been floated as a potential 2028 presidential candidate, Whitmer said her focus remains on serving Michigan and helping her party’s candidates win midterm contests as the vice president of the Democratic Governor’s Association.  

In response to a question about how Democrats can win in the midterms, Whitmer reflected on her winning gubernatorial campaign’s decision to stay “focused on the fundamentals.”

“I don’t think Michigan is unique in that,” Whitmer said. “I think every person in this country wants and expects government to make their lives better, and so that’s been our formula here in Michigan and I think that can be replicated everywhere successfully.”
